verses.commentary: The problem stated again. Ten years in Canaan (verse 3) and still childless. Hagar is introduced as Egyptian, recalling the Egypt episode (chapter 12). She may have been acquired there.
Sarai's plan. She blames God. Sleep with my slave was culturally acceptable surrogacy. Sarai takes initiative. Abram passively agrees. This is faithlessness disguised as pragmatism.
Ten years emphasizes the long wait. Hagar becomes a wife, not just a surrogate. This complicates the household. Taking matters into their own hands rather than waiting on God's timing.
Immediate conception. Hagar's status changes. She despises Sarai, treating her lightly or with contempt. Pregnancy gives Hagar a sense of superiority. Sarai's barrenness is implicitly highlighted.
Sarai blames Abram. Ironic, since it was her plan. Domestic strife erupts. May the LORD judge invokes divine arbitration. The household fractures under the weight of human scheming.

Abram abdicates responsibility. Do with her whatever you think best is cowardice. Sarai mistreated Hagar, the same word used for Israel's oppression in Egypt (15:13). Hagar flees into the wilderness. The plan collapses into abuse and flight.
First appearance of the angel of the LORD. This mysterious figure often represents God himself. Hagar is found, not lost. The spring is life-giving. Shur leads back to Egypt. Hagar is fleeing home.
The angel asks questions to prompt self-awareness. Hagar identifies herself as slave of Sarai. She admits she is running away. Honesty about her situation opens the door to divine intervention.
Hard command. Return to oppression. Submit uses the same root as mistreated. God asks her to endure difficult circumstances. This is not endorsement of abuse but a call to trust God in hardship.
Promise to Hagar. Language parallel to Abram's promise (13:16, 15:5). Hagar receives her own covenant. Her descendants will be innumerable. God sees and blesses the oppressed.
Prophetic announcement. A son. Ishmael means God hears. The name commemorates her suffering. The LORD has heard your misery. God listens to the afflicted.

Ishmael's character foretold. Wild donkey suggests fierce independence, untamed. His hand against everyone describes conflict. Hostility toward his brothers predicts ongoing tension between Ishmael's and Isaac's descendants. This is description, not prescription.

Hagar names God. El Roi, God who sees me. Remarkable. An Egyptian slave woman gives God a name. I have now seen the One who sees me expresses wonder. She encountered God directly. God sees the marginalized.
The well is named. Beer Lahai Roi means well of the Living One who sees me. Geography becomes theology. The place commemorates the encounter.
Hagar returns and gives birth. Abram names him Ishmael, showing acceptance of the angelic message. The son is born but is not the son of promise.
Chronology. Abram is 86. Eleven years since the promise in chapter 12. Fourteen more years until Isaac (21:5). The wait continues.
